reports.title: Ares Management Corp. (ARES): ARCC read-across: Slower deployment and modest pick up in non-accruals
reports.headline: GS says ARCC's 1Q26 print points to slower direct-lending deployment, muted 2Q activity, and a modest uptick in credit stress, but scaled managers like ARES should benefit when activity normalizes.
reports.summary: Goldman Sachs reiterates its positive view on ARES, using ARCC's 1Q26 results as a read-across for ARES' credit business. The main takeaways are a sharp decel in net commitments/deployment, muted April activity, and a modest rise in non-accruals, partly offset by still-solid credit metrics and fee contribution. GS expects NT direct-lending deployment to stay soft amid volatility, but sees ARES as well positioned to redeploy its large dry powder when markets reopen.
important_observations: Direct-lending activity appears to have slowed materially versus recent qtrs, with net commitments nearly stalling in 1Q26.
April activity remained muted and backlog declined, suggesting 2Q deployment may stay soft absent better market conditions.
Non-accruals moved higher but remain within historical ranges, keeping focus on manager-by-manager credit dispersion.
Scaled platforms like ARES may gain share when origination rebounds given roughly $100bn of available credit capital.
Portfolio EBITDA softened q/q and leverage ticked up, but comparable TTM EBITDA growth and interest coverage were still supportive.
new_data: ARCC gross new commitments were $3,246mn in 1Q26 vs $5,825mn in 4Q25 and $3,453mn y/y.
Net new commitments fell to $70mn in 1Q26 vs $1,164mn in 4Q25 and $596mn y/y.
Net fundings were $277mn in 1Q26 vs $515mn in 4Q25 and $243mn y/y.
Through Apr. 23, gross new commitments were about $198mn vs exits/repayments of about $13mn.
Backlog was about $1.8bn on Apr. 23, down from $2.2bn at end-Jan. 2026.
Weighted avg yield on debt investments funded in 1Q26 was 9.2% vs 9.9% in 1Q25.
Non-accruals rose 30 bps q/q to 2.1% of cost; non-accruals were 1.2% of fair value, flat q/q.
Portfolio weighted avg EBITDA fell 5% q/q to $291mn from $307mn.
Portfolio weighted avg net lev increased to 5.7x from 5.6x.
ARCC management fees were $111mn, about 3% above GS's ARES model.
management_insights: Mgmt indicated muted activity so far in 2Q, consistent with recent market volatility.
Mgmt disclosed less than $200mn of commitments in April, well below roughly $500mn in Apr. 2025.
Mgmt showed backlog down to about $1.8bn by Apr. 23 from $2.2bn at end-Jan., signaling a lighter near-term pipeline.
Read-through implies credit performance is beginning to diverge more across managers, making asset selection and scale more important.
evidence_quality: Primary evidence: ARCC 1Q26 company-reported results, 10-Q, and KPI disclosures.
Primary evidence: month-to-date Apr. 23 operating datapoints and backlog commentary from mgmt/company materials.
Triangulated read-across: GS maps ARCC trends to ARES because ARCC drives 30%+ of ARES Credit segment management fees.
Mostly observational/analyst inference on NT deployment outlook for ARES and alt managers; limited new disclosed ARES-specific est revisions in the note.
risks_or_watch_items: 2Q direct-lending deployment could remain muted if volatility persists.
Further increases in non-accruals or weaker borrower fundamentals could pressure sentiment on ARES credit earnings.
Credit performance may diverge more across managers, increasing execution risk.
Backlog erosion and lower new-money yields/exits mix could limit fee and earnings momentum.
GS flags wider credit spreads and slower-than-expected realizations as downside risks.
key_numbers: ARES rating/PT: Buy, $131 PT; price $112.18; implied upside 16.8%.
ARCC contributes 30%+ of ARES Credit segment management fees, incl. Part I fees.
Available credit capital at ARES: about $100bn.
Apr. 23 MTD gross commitments: about $198mn; exits/repayments: about $13mn.
Apr. 23 backlog: about $1.8bn vs $2.2bn at end-Jan. 2026.
1Q26 funded yield: 9.2%; Apr. funded yield: 9.1%; yield on exited/repaid investments: 9.7%.
Implied spread on new fundings: about 550 bps.
Non-accruals: 2.1% of cost and 1.2% of fair value.
ARCC management fees: $111mn; Part I fees: $84mn.

reports.title: Asbury Automotive: 1Q26 Miss on New/Used Units, GPUs and P&S Better; Expect Underperformance Today; Sector Read Mixed as GPUs Better and SS Unit Weakness Likely Due to DMS Transition
reports.companies_covered: ABG, AN, GPI, LAD, PAG, SAH
reports.headline: ABG 1Q26 missed on units and EPS, with JPM pointing to divestitures and Tekion DMS disruption as key drags; GPUs and P&S were relatively better, giving a somewhat less negative read-through for peers than feared.
reports.summary: JPM says ABG’s 1Q26 print was a sizable miss, with EPS 8% below JPM and 4% below cons as new/used unit weakness, SS GP underperformance, and weaker SG&A leverage more than offset better TCA income and lower floorplan interest. The analyst views the sharp SS unit miss as likely idiosyncratic and tied to the ongoing Tekion DMS transition, while better new/used GPUs and P&S trends provide a modestly constructive sector signal. JPM expects ABG shares to underperform near term and sees the broader dealer group trading lower until the market gets more clarity on how much of the weakness is DMS-related versus fundamental.
important_observations: Unit weakness appears more severe than gross profit weakness, suggesting operational disruption rather than purely pricing deterioration.
Better new and used GPUs plus solid P&S temper the negative read-through for peers despite ABG's headline EPS miss.
SS gross profit fell 8% y/y, worse than JPM's -6%, pointing to incremental pressure beyond divestitures alone.
Elevated divestiture activity distorted the qtr and complicates clean read-through on underlying demand/productivity.
Sector setup into dealer earnings may be less negative than feared if ABG's SS unit miss is mainly DMS-specific.
new_data: 1Q26 EPS was $5.37 vs JPM $5.85 and cons roughly $5.59.
Adj EBITDA was $207 mn vs JPM $218 mn.
Total retail automotive rev was $4,113 mn vs JPM $4,434 mn.
Net income was $102 mn vs JPM $109 mn.
New vehicle units were 39,282 vs JPM 42,990; used vehicle units were 33,202 vs JPM 35,627.
New GPU was $3,271 vs JPM $3,246; used GPU was $1,849 vs JPM $1,759; F&I GPU was $2,303 vs JPM $2,273.
P&S rev was $365 mn vs JPM $368 mn, with SS P&S GP growth -1% vs JPM -2%.
ABG bought back ~$147 mn of stock in 1Q26.
ABG divested 10 stores and terminated 7 franchises across 3 stores, representing ~$625 mn annualized rev and ~$210 mn cash inflow.
evidence_quality: Primary source: company-reported 1Q26 results, with detailed metric-by-metric comparison vs JPM model.
Analyst model work: conclusions on miss drivers, sector read-through, and implied peer EPS are partly inference-based.
Moderate triangulation: peer read-through uses ABG GPU data extrapolated across AN/GPI/LAD/PAG/SAH, which is useful but indirect.
No substantive mgmt call commentary yet in this note; several key issues are framed as questions for the 10am ET call.
Weight near-term datapoints fairly highly for quarter mechanics, but use caution on broader thesis until DMS disruption and divestiture effects are better isolated.
risks_or_watch_items: Tekion DMS integration path and speed of productivity recovery at converted stores.
Whether SS unit weakness persists beyond the transition period or proves temporary/idiosyncratic.
2026 SG&A/GP guardrails and scope for incremental cost actions.
Capital allocation priorities and de-leveraging timeline after divestitures.
Forward trajectory for new GPUs, used unit volumes, and P&S growth.
Potential sympathy pressure across dealer stocks until investors gain confidence on peer read-through.
key_numbers: EPS: $5.37 vs JPM $5.85, 4% below cons and 8% below JPM
Adj EBITDA: $207 mn vs JPM $218 mn
Net income: $102 mn vs JPM $109 mn
Total retail automotive rev: $4,113 mn vs JPM $4,434 mn
Gross profit: $727 mn vs JPM $756 mn
Operating income: $206 mn vs JPM $219 mn
SS GP growth: -8% y/y vs JPM -6% y/y
Divestitures: ~$625 mn annualized rev; ~$210 mn cash inflow

reports.title: 1Q26 Ahead Across the Board with Guidance Increased; Assumptions Around ACA Exchanges and Medicaid Progression in Focus
reports.headline: CNC posted a broad 1Q26 beat and raised FY26 adj. EPS guide, but JPM stays Neutral as investors focus on ACA Exchange acuity, Medicaid progression, and whether the 1Q beat was mostly timing/seasonality.
reports.summary: JPM says CNC delivered a clear 1Q26 beat across Medicaid, Medicare, and Commercial, with adj. EPS of $3.37 vs $2.28 cons and FY26 adj. EPS guide raised to >$3.40 from >$3.00. The note frames the key debate around how much of the beat is sustainable versus seasonal/cadence-driven, especially in ACA Exchange where higher Silver-tier acuity is expected to be offset later by 2026 risk adjustment. JPM views the quarter as a positive start but remains focused on the assumptions embedded in guidance over the next two qtrs.
important_observations: Guide raise of $0.40 trails a >$1.00 1Q EPS beat, suggesting mgmt conservatism or meaningful seasonality/cadence in the rest of FY26.
Commercial outperformance appears to be the largest swing factor, but JPM questions how much should flow through given likely seasonality.
ACA Exchange remains the central debate, with higher Silver-tier acuity and specialty Rx util creating uncertainty until 2026 risk adjustment benefits are realized.
Medicaid trends were better than feared, supported by rate/rev increases, med cost management, and moderate flu costs.
Medicare margin progression continued, aided by lapping the 1Q25 PDP drag and better HBR.
Reaffirmed FY26 HBR despite the beat implies a steeper cost-ratio deterioration through the year, which keeps investors focused on back-half execution.
new_data: 1Q26 adj. EPS $3.37 vs JPM $2.34 and cons $2.28.
1Q26 consolidated HBR 87.3% vs JPM 88.9% and cons 89.2%.
Medicaid HBR 93.1% vs JPM 93.4% and cons 93.6%.
Medicare HBR 84.9% vs JPM 85.9% and cons 86.9%.
Commercial HBR 75.3% vs cons 79.1%.
Adj. SG&A 7.6% vs JPM/cons 7.4%.
Investment income $407m vs JPM/cons $351m.
FY26 adj. EPS guide raised to >$3.40 from >$3.00.
FY26 premium revenue guide raised by about $1bn; adj. SG&A midpoint lowered about 10 bps; investment income guide raised about $50m.
DCP was 48 days vs 46 days in 4Q25.
management_insights: Mgmt said the 1Q beat was about $0.50 better than internal expectations, implying part of the upside may be timing-related.
Mgmt highlighted favorable y/y Medicaid impact from rate and rev increases, tangible progress on med cost management, and moderate flu cost.
Mgmt said Commercial/Marketplace results reflected higher acuity among Silver-tier members before anticipated 2026 net risk-adjustment benefit.
Mgmt left FY26 HBR guidance unchanged despite the quarter, indicating caution around the rest of the year.
Mgmt improved SG&A assumptions and investment income outlook as part of the FY26 guide update.

estimate_change_details.note: Company raised FY26 adj. EPS guide; new floor is 40c higher.

estimate_change_details.period: FY26 premium and service revenue guidance midpoint
estimate_change_details.note: Prior midpoint inferred from company comment that premium revenue guidance increased by about $1bn.
Report states investment income guidance was increased by about $50m, but prior absolute guide was not provided.

evidence_quality: Primary: company-reported 1Q26 results and updated FY26 guidance.
Primary: mgmt commentary from earnings release, with additional focus on upcoming call assumptions.
Triangulated: JPM compares reported metrics against its model and Bloomberg cons, giving solid context on magnitude of beat/miss.
Partly inference-based: views on seasonality, cadence, and conservatism are analyst interpretation rather than fully disclosed company data.
risks_or_watch_items: ACA Exchange morbidity and specialty Rx util, especially among Silver-tier members.
Whether anticipated 2026 risk-adjustment benefit materializes as expected.
Medicaid rate updates and underlying med cost trend through the year.
Durability of Commercial margin upside versus seasonal benefit.
MA util trends and commentary around final 2027 rates.
PDP margin sustainability after a strong 2025.
Back-half FY26 HBR cadence implied by unchanged HBR guide despite 1Q beat.
key_numbers: FY26 adj. EPS guide: >$3.40 vs >$3.00 prior
FY26 premium and service revenue guide midpoint: $173.0bn

reports.title: 1Q26 First Look: Strong EPS Beat and Raise Reinforces ACA Recovery
reports.headline: CNC 1Q26 EPS beat by ~50% vs cons, driven by broad MLR outperformance; FY26 EPS guide raised by $0.40, supporting ACA recovery thesis.
reports.summary: Barclays frames CNC's 1Q26 print as a material beat-and-raise that should ease ACA margin concerns and reinforce the recovery case. Adj. EPS of $3.37 beat cons by ~48-50%, primarily on better-than-expected MLR across Commercial/ACA, Medicare, and Medicaid, while FY26 EPS guidance was lifted by $0.40 to >$3.40. Barclays highlights especially strong ACA and Medicare MLR performance, modestly better Medicaid enrollment, and commentary that pricing remains conservative despite higher-acuity silver-book utilization, leaving room for possible 2Q benefit from risk adjustment.
important_observations: ACA MLR beat was strong enough to absorb the specialty pharmacy headwind flagged in Mar, supporting the view that pricing conservatism remains embedded.
Broad-based MLR upside across all segments reduces concern that 1Q strength was isolated or timing-driven.
Mgmt said 1Q exceeded internal expectations by $0.50, implying the beat was not only vs Street numbers but also ahead of internal planning.
Updated guidance does not include any benefit from higher ACA acuity risk adjustment, creating a potential 2Q upside lever.
Medicaid margin performance benefited from both favorable seasonal flu dynamics and more durable drivers like rate increases and medical cost management.
Enrollment progression looked broadly consistent with prior guide after adjusting for the MDwise exit effect, lowering risk of a major membership disconnect.
new_data: 1Q26 adj. EPS $3.37 vs cons $2.28; +16% y/y.
FY26 EPS guidance raised by $0.40 to >$3.40.
Total operating rev $49.9B, +7.1% y/y, 5.6% above cons.
MLR 87.3% vs cons 89.2%, 197 bps better than cons.
Medicaid MLR 93.1% vs cons 93.6%, 53 bps better.
Commercial/ACA MLR 75.3% vs cons 79.6%, 431 bps better.
Medicare MLR 84.9% vs cons 86.9%, 197 bps better.
Net earnings attributable to CNC $1.7B vs cons $1.1B.
Medicaid enrollment 12.427M, down 0.7% q/q, 0.7% above cons.
Commercial Marketplace membership 3.582M; total paid ACA membership consistent with Mar disclosure.
PYD reserve development $1.194B, contributing 10 bps to enterprise MLR.
Days claims payable 48 days, down 1 day y/y and up 2 days q/q.
management_insights: Mgmt said 1Q results exceeded internal expectations by $0.50.
Mgmt cited milder flu season as a benefit to Medicaid MLR.
Mgmt also pointed to rate increases and medical cost management initiatives as Medicaid margin drivers.
Mgmt said ACA MLR ran slightly above expectations due to higher acuity in the silver membership book, including elevated specialty pharmacy trend.
Updated guidance excludes any benefit from ACA risk adjustment tied to higher acuity, which Barclays sees as a possible 2Q tailwind.

estimate_change_details.note: Company raised full-year EPS guidance by $0.40; prior implied from new guide minus stated increase.

evidence_quality: Primary: company-reported 1Q26 results and guidance update; highest weight for reported P&L, MLR, and membership datapoints.
Primary + mgmt commentary: quarter update includes specific explanations on flu, rate actions, cost management, and ACA acuity.
Triangulated: Barclays compares results against Bloomberg consensus and its own model, which helps quantify the beat but remains partly analyst framing.

Some inference: estimated flu benefit (~15 bps), specialty pharmacy impact (~40 bps), MDwise effect (~60k members), and possible 2Q risk-adjustment upside are analyst-derived rather than company-confirmed.
risks_or_watch_items: Need to monitor whether ACA specialty pharmacy trend persists or worsens beyond what pricing can absorb.
2Q risk-adjustment outcome is a watch item since guide excludes benefit and could move earnings either way depending on final acuity settlement.
Medicaid margin durability should be tested beyond the favorable flu backdrop.
Membership attrition in Medicaid and the gap between OEP and paid ACA enrollment remain important for revenue and margin progression.
Downside risks cited by Barclays include higher medical/pharmacy cost trend, slower Medicaid/ACA margin recovery, customer losses, and weaker cost savings realization.
key_numbers: Net earnings attributable to CNC $1.7B vs cons $1.1B.
Medicaid MLR 93.1%; Commercial/ACA MLR 75.3%; Medicare MLR 84.9%.

reports.title: 1Q26 Earnings Preview: Delivering a Positive Narrative Shift
reports.headline: DB stays Buy/PT $290; sees 1Q26 near high end of guide with AWS accel offsetting softer ads and raises 2026/2027 EPS on stronger AWS profit outlook.
reports.summary: DB previews AMZN 1Q26 with rev/OI roughly in line with cons but near the high end of mgmt guide, supported by resilient NA ecom trends and improving AWS demand/consumption. The main change is a more constructive AWS narrative: DB cites stronger commitments, Bedrock/Trainium traction, large AI customer wins, and better chip supply visibility, which more than offsets softer ad checks and a 2% cut to FY26/FY27 ad rev fcst. DB reiterates Buy and $290 PT, and raises 2026/2027 EPS by 4.5%/5.4%.
important_observations: Variant view is that Street may be underestimating AWS momentum while over-focusing on AI-related margin dilution risk.
DB thinks NA retail cons may be achievable to conservative despite macro concerns, with non-store retail and app activity suggesting 1Q improved vs 4Q.
Ad growth appears to be decelerating in core Sponsored Products, but DSP mix is stronger and agency checks point to q/q re-accel in 2Q26.
AWS margin debate remains key into the print; DB expects 1Q margin compression to bottom and modest y/y improvement in 2Q26.
Recent AI/chip announcements weaken the 'AMZN AI laggard' narrative and reduce concerns around GPU supply constraints.
Globalstar acquisition is framed as strategically accretive for Leo/Kuiper, spectrum access, edge compute, and Apple-related recurring rev.
new_data: AWS AI rev run-rate >$15bn as of 1Q26 per Andy Jassy shareholder letter.
AMZN chips business run-rate >$20bn, growing triple digits y/y; Trainium2 largely sold out, Trainium3 nearly fully subscribed, part of Trainium4 already reserved.
Archera checks: AWS NTM committed dollar growth rose from 28% in Dec to 30% in Feb, with March seen higher.
Archera checks: AWS consumption growth reached 32.5% y/y in Feb, up 3.5 pts.
Nvidia disclosed a multi-year deal with AWS for ~1mn AI processors by end-2027.
AWS/OpenAI partnership includes up to $50bn investment and Bedrock enterprise distribution.
Anthropic to spend $100bn+ on AWS tech over 10 years and secured up to 5GW of capacity.
AMZN announced Globalstar acquisition at $90/share, or about $12bn.
management_insights: Jassy emphasized AI and infra investment in the Apr. 9 shareholder letter, highlighting rapid AI scaling at AWS.
Mgmt commentary implies strong demand for Trainium and Bedrock, with future chip capacity already heavily reserved.
AWS custom silicon is positioned as a price/performance advantage, with inference increasingly running on Trainium.
Company implemented a 3.5% FBA fuel/logistics surcharge effective Apr. 17 to offset higher fuel costs.

estimate_change_details.note: DB lowered FY26 advertising revenue fcst by 2%, but absolute prior/new values were not disclosed.
DB lowered FY27 advertising revenue fcst by 2%, but absolute prior/new values were not disclosed.
DB raised FY26 EPS est by 4.5%, but absolute prior/new values were not disclosed.
DB raised FY27 EPS est by 5.4%, but absolute prior/new values were not disclosed.
PT maintained at $290.

evidence_quality: Primary: company-reported guide and Jassy shareholder letter provide direct evidence on 1Q rev/OI range, AWS AI scale, and chip demand.
Triangulated alt data: Second Measure, Sensor Tower, non-store retail data, and ad agency checks support the ecom/ads read-through.
Channel/expert checks: Archera commentary on AWS commitments and consumption is useful but still anecdotal and customer-base specific.
Market/industry data: Nvidia GTC disclosures, WSJ-reported OpenAI/Anthropic spending plans, and announced customer wins strengthen the AWS demand case.
Analyst model work: rev/OI/margin conclusions and EPS revisions rely partly on DB inference, especially around AWS margin trajectory and ad softness.
risks_or_watch_items: AWS OI margin on AI workloads remains a central debate and could matter more than top-line upside.
Ad rev decel in Sponsored Products could worsen if macro or advertiser demand softens further.
Middle East conflict and higher gas prices may pressure consumer demand and delivery costs.
FX became less of a tailwind in 1Q and could modestly weigh reported results.
Execution/regulatory risk on Globalstar deal and on satellite replacement milestones through 2027.
Street expectations for AWS may be moving higher into the print, raising the bar for upside.
key_numbers: Buy; PT $290; stock price $248.28 on Apr. 20, 2026.
1Q26 Street NA retail growth est 9.9% y/y; 2Q26 Street 9.3% y/y.
AWS margin est: 35.2% in 1Q26 vs 39.5% in 1Q25; 33.1% in 2Q26 vs 32.9% in 2Q25.
AWS AI rev run-rate >$15bn; chips business run-rate >$20bn.
Anthropic AWS commitment $100bn+ over 10 years; up to 5GW capacity.
DB says FY26/FY27 ad rev fcst cut 2%; FY26/FY27 EPS est raised 4.5%/5.4%.

reports.title: Ares Management: Model Update: Institutional Centric Funding Model Positions Ares Well To Gain Share, However Not Immune From Macro And Sentiment
reports.headline: JPM keeps OW on ARES but cuts Dec-26 PT to $144 from $188 and trims 26E/27E EPS on slower DL activity, softer private wealth flows, and lower sector multiples.
reports.summary: JPM updates ARES ahead of 1Q26 and stays constructive on the firm's institutional/fee-centric model, diversified credit platform, and real assets growth runway, but cuts ests and PT to reflect a weaker macro/dealmaking backdrop, slower U.S. direct lending wealth flows, lower BDC fundraising, and sector de-rating. The note argues ARES can still gain share in dislocation and benefit from institutional fundraising plus GCP-driven real assets expansion, but near-term deployment, leverage, and sentiment are likely softer than previously modeled.
important_observations: ARES's funding mix remains more institutional and management-fee driven than peers, supporting steadier earnings despite macro noise.
JPM sees ARES's broad credit platform as a share gainer in dislocated markets, but 1H26 deployment likely decels vs 2H25 peaks.
Private wealth softness appears concentrated in U.S. direct lending; non-DL strategies and Europe look more resilient.
Real Assets is framed as a key growth offset, helped by GCP, expected 2026-27 fund launches, and better RE/perf fee setup.
The PT cut is driven not just by lower EPS but by a meaningful multiple reset, implying sector sentiment/risk premia matter as much as fundamentals.
new_data: Global M&A was $1.68tn in 1Q26; April announced/closed volume was only $351bn, signaling a softer 2Q26 pace.
JPM credit team estimates Mar direct lending vols fell 29% y/y to $20.7bn; 1Q26 DL volume was $61.1bn, down 14% y/y.
1Q26 LBO-funded DL deployment was $23.3bn, down 17% y/y.
JPM lowers 2026 Credit segment FPAUM commitments to $26bn from $29bn.
JPM now models Credit deployment/leverage at $8.0bn in 1Q26 and $8.5bn in 2Q26 vs $9.5bn in 4Q25 and $9.9bn in 3Q25.
JPM now models 2026 FRE margin expansion of ~108 bps, mid-range of ARES's typical 0-150 bps annual walk.
Real Assets FRPR ests move to $114mm for 2026 from $98mm prior; 2027 moves to $168mm from $259mm prior.
2026 net carry est is cut to $351mm from $401mm prior; JPM's 1Q26 net carry est is $78mm after mgmt tempered near-term outlook.
management_insights: Mgmt has said the firm is seeing attractive opportunities across most business lines despite macro volatility.
Mgmt previously indicated 2026 capital collections should be at least as strong as record 2025 levels of $113bn.
Mgmt has guided to further FRE margin expansion, with 2026 at the high end of the normal 0-150 bps annual range.
Mgmt indicated Alternative Credit Pathfinder III should complete fundraising by end of summer.
Mgmt had flagged $52mm of European waterfall net carry slipping from 4Q25 into 1Q26 as a timing item and previously pointed to roughly $200mm of net carry across 4Q25-1Q26.
As of Mar 31 filings, mgmt tempered firmwide 1Q26 net carry outlook to $75mm and reiterated $350mm for full-year 2026.

estimate_change_details.note: Key Changes table; cut on softer deployment/fundraising and macro uncertainty.
Key Changes table; more modest haircut than 2026E.
PT cut reflects lower FRE multiple and broader market risk, not only lower ests.
Units are $bn; lowered for slower U.S. private wealth/direct lending flows.
Units are $mm; better non-traded REIT/perf fee outlook.
Units are $mm; still above 2025 actuals, but reset lower vs prior model.
Units are $mm; reflects less robust asset growth outlook for BDC vehicles.
Units are $mm; continued moderation in retail fundraising assumptions.
Units are $mm; incorporates softer carry expectations despite some 1Q26 timing benefits.

evidence_quality: Primary: company-reported results/filings, prior earnings call commentary, and mgmt guidance are core inputs; high weight on disclosed fee, carry, and fundraising commentary.
Triangulated: JPM combines company data with Bloomberg global M&A data and internal HY/LL credit team estimates on DL volumes; moderate-to-high weight.
Market/industry data: private wealth flow slowdown and non-traded BDC redemption trends rely partly on broader market tracker work; useful but somewhat inferential.
Analyst model work: PT, multiple reset, deployment assumptions, and est revisions are mostly analyst inference layered on primary data; medium weight.
Not based on channel checks/expert call/survey in the text; conclusions are mainly fundamentals + market data, not anecdotal evidence.
risks_or_watch_items: 2Q26 M&A/dealmaking decel could pressure deployment, fee activation timing, and sentiment.
Further slowdown in U.S. private wealth/direct lending flows and rising non-traded BDC redemptions could weigh on Credit FPAUM growth.
Credit quality deterioration remains the key downside risk for returns, fundraising, and valuation.
GCP integration execution is still a watch item given deal size and higher acquisition multiple vs prior M&A.
Carry timing/outlook remains volatile; 1Q26 and FY26 realization cadence needs monitoring against mgmt's $350mm guide.
Sector multiple compression could persist even if ARES fundamentals hold up relatively better than peers.
key_numbers: Rating/PT: OW maintained; PT cut to $144 from $188.
Adj. EPS: 2026E cut to $5.98 from $6.49; 2027E cut to $7.52 from $7.76.
1Q26 global M&A: $1.68tn; April M&A to date: $351bn.
1Q26 DL volume: $61.1bn, down 14% y/y; Mar DL volume: $20.7bn, down 29% y/y.
2026 Credit FPAUM commitments: cut to $26bn from $29bn.
2026 net carry est: $351mm vs $401mm prior; 1Q26 net carry est: $78mm.
2026 FRE margin expansion modeled at ~108 bps.

reports.title: Oil Flash Note: Simple math
reports.headline: JPM argues the oil market is clearing via forced demand loss and heavy inventory draws after a Hormuz-related supply shock, with further price upside likely needed to balance the market.
reports.summary: J.P. Morgan frames this as a macro commodities flash note rather than single-stock research, arguing that a roughly 14 mbd supply disruption tied to the Strait of Hormuz closure has overwhelmed normal balancing mechanisms because Gulf spare capacity is effectively inaccessible. The note says the market has instead adjusted through massive inventory draws and forced demand loss, especially in the Middle East, Asia, and Africa, with Europe and potentially the US still needing to absorb more of the rebalancing. JPM’s key conclusion is that current prices have not yet done enough work to clear the market globally, implying further tightening and higher prices may be required.
important_observations: What looks like demand destruction is, in JPM's view, largely forced demand loss caused by missing physical supply rather than normal price elasticity.
The usual first shock absorber, Gulf spare capacity, is effectively unavailable, making this disruption structurally harder to offset than a typical supply outage.
The remaining balancing gap appears too large for EM demand weakness alone, implying Europe and the US may still need to absorb more rationing via higher prices.
Petchem feedstocks appear to be the earliest and largest point of adjustment, suggesting downstream industrial weakness could deepen before broader transport fuels fully roll over.
The Americas are relatively insulated near term, but JPM flags early signs that higher pump prices and airfares are beginning to dent US driving and jet demand.
new_data: JPM estimates global supply disruption at 9.1 mbd in March, widening to 13.7 mbd in April.
Observable commercial and strategic inventories drew 4.0 mbd in March and 7.1 mbd in April.
Observed global oil demand fell 2.8 mbd in March and is tracking down 4.3 mbd in April.
Middle East, Asia, and Africa account for 87% of the 4.3 mbd April demand hit.
Feedstock-driven weakness represents about 55% of April demand loss; jet fuel is about 11% of the loss.
Russian supply is said to be down 350 kbd in recent weeks, while Russia's spare capacity is only about 300 kbd.
US shale response is limited near term, with only 0.3-0.7 mbd likely over 3-6 months and up to 1 mbd over 6-12 months.
India March LPG consumption fell 13% YoY, cited as evidence of feedstock/fuel stress.
evidence_quality: Company-reported/public market data: draws on Kpler, IEA, EIA, JODI, Singapore, PAJ, Bloomberg, and Rystad; evidence base is largely market/industry data rather than company-specific disclosures.
Analyst model work: core conclusions rely on JPM's balance math and inferred global demand-loss estimates; a meaningful portion is analyst synthesis rather than directly observed data.
Primary vs inference: inventory data are partly observable, but JPM explicitly notes incomplete visibility, especially in product stocks, so true draws may differ materially.
Triangulated but not fully verifiable in real time: the note combines multiple datasets and market price signals, but several conclusions depend on interpreting shortages and residual balancing gaps.
No mgmt meeting/call, channel checks, or survey evidence is cited; weight should be placed on macro balance analysis, with caution around fast-moving disruption assumptions.
risks_or_watch_items: Further price upside may be needed if Europe and the US must participate more fully in demand rationing.
Inventory visibility is incomplete, especially for product stocks, creating risk that actual draws are either larger or less sustainable than observed.
Jet demand could weaken further in May as airlines across Asia and Europe cut activity.
Diesel and jet markets in Europe already appear tight; additional refinery constraints could broaden product stress into gasoline during the US summer driving season.
A slower-than-expected non-Gulf supply response, especially from US shale, would keep the market tighter for longer.
Regional dislocations in Asia and Africa could intensify if cargoes continue to be diverted toward higher-bidding markets.
key_numbers: Supply disruption: 9.1 mbd in March; 13.7 mbd in April.
Observable inventory draws: 4.0 mbd in March; 7.1 mbd in April.
Demand loss: 2.8 mbd in March; 4.3 mbd in April.
Residual balancing need: about 2 mbd even assuming an aggressive 8 mbd inventory contribution.
Regional concentration: 87% of April demand hit in Middle East, Asia, and Africa.
Feedstocks share of April demand loss: about 55%; jet fuel share: 11%.
Brent futures avg: just under $100/bbl in March and April to date; dated crude avg: $107 in March and $123 in April to date.
US shale response: 0.3-0.7 mbd over 3-6 months; up to 1.0 mbd over 6-12 months.
Russia spare capacity: about 300 kbd; recent Russian supply decline: 350 kbd.
India LPG consumption: down 13% YoY in March.

reports.title: Rise of the AI Agent - Global Implications
reports.headline: Agentic AI broadens the AI trade from GPUs to full-stack infra, with CPU orchestration and memory becoming the next major bottlenecks.
reports.summary: Morgan Stanley argues agentic AI shifts value creation from standalone accelerators toward system orchestration, lifting CPU, DRAM, packaging, foundry and infra content across the stack. The note frames CPU as the control plane for multi-step AI workflows and estimates agentic workloads can add $32.5-60bn of incremental CPU TAM by 2030 within a $100bn+ server CPU market, alongside 15-45EB of incremental DRAM demand. The firm highlights global beneficiaries across memory, substrates, foundry, BMC/memory interface, storage and semi-cap, and raises PTs on key Korea names tied to memory and ABF exposure.
important_observations: The bottleneck is shifting from raw GPU throughput to orchestration, data movement and memory hierarchy, which broadens AI upside beyond accelerators.
CPU-side orchestration is framed as the dominant latency source in agentic workloads, implying structurally higher cluster CPU intensity and a rising CPU:GPU ratio.
Memory is presented as an active architectural layer rather than passive storage, supporting persistent context, recall and continuous learning.
Morgan Stanley prefers exposure via enablers where capex/token growth maps more directly into earnings upside, especially memory, foundry, ABF and infrastructure ICs.
Supply-constrained layers such as leading-edge foundry, ABF substrates, BMCs, memory interconnect and equipment are positioned to capture outsized economics as system complexity rises.
The note suggests agentic AI can extend the storage cycle as persistent context and knowledge layers increase HDD and NAND demand, not just DRAM.
new_data: Introduces a new Morgan Stanley framework to size CPU and memory upside from agentic AI.
Estimates incremental CPU TAM of $32.5-60bn by 2030.
Estimates total server CPU TAM of $100bn+ by 2030; executive summary cites $82.5-110bn.
Estimates 15-45EB of incremental DRAM demand by 2030, equal to 26-77% of 2027 annual DRAM supply.
States CPU-side orchestration can represent 50-90% of total workload latency.
Estimates agentic AI could drive 5-10% incremental ABF substrate demand upside by 2030 from enlarged CPU TAM alone.
Highlights a shift in server architecture from roughly 1 CPU:12 GPU historically toward 1:8, 1:2, and potentially 2:1 in newer platforms.
management_insights: Cites NVIDIA CEO Jensen Huang at GTC 2026 saying the CPU is increasingly driving AI systems rather than merely supporting them.
Notes NVIDIA expects standalone CPUs to become a multi-billion-dollar business over time, with Meta and CoreWeave cited as early customers.
Says GUC mgmt indicated next-gen Arm-based CPU design wins with major customers including Google and Microsoft.
Frames industry positioning as increasingly shaped by hyperscaler demand for vertically integrated, AI-optimized compute platforms.

estimate_change_details.note: SK hynix PT raised.
Samsung Electronics common PT raised.
Samsung Electronics preferred PT raised.
Samsung Electro-Mechanics PT raised.

evidence_quality: Analyst model work: core TAM, latency and architecture conclusions are primarily Morgan Stanley estimates and framework-driven; useful for scenario analysis but not primary evidence.
Market/industry data: includes references to Mercury Research, IDC, Prismark and FactSet-style market/share data, which adds external support but still requires interpretation.
Management/public commentary: uses public statements and roadmaps from NVIDIA and others; relevant directional evidence, but not equivalent to company-reported financial guidance.
Channel checks: supply tightness, pricing power and capacity-constraint views are partly supported by channel checks; informative but inherently anecdotal.
Mostly triangulated and inference-heavy rather than company-reported results; weight should be moderate for thematic positioning, lower for precise sizing.
risks_or_watch_items: Actual CPU attach and CPU:GPU ratio progression in production agentic clusters may lag the report's framework.
Leading-edge foundry, ABF and memory supply constraints could limit volume realization even if demand materializes.
Some stock calls depend on outer-year capex and architecture shifts that remain partly roadmap-based rather than booked demand.
Competitive dynamics across NVIDIA, AMD, Arm and Intel may change faster than assumed as specialized AI CPUs evolve.
TCO and enterprise adoption curves for agentic workloads remain uncertain and could slow monetization of the projected TAM.
key_numbers: 26-77% of 2027 DRAM supply from incremental agentic demand
50-90% of workload latency attributed to CPU-side orchestration
Samsung Electronics pref PT: W213,350 -> W289,600

reports.title: CY26 Data Center Capex Revised Higher and Strong Initial Growth Outlook of 40% for CY27; Positive Across the Semiconductor AI Value Chain - Potential for Continued Upward Revisions
reports.companies_covered: AMD, ADI, AMAT, ARM, ALAB, AVGO, CDNS, KLAC, LRCX, MTSI, MRVL, MU, NVDA, SNPS
reports.headline: JPM raises CY26 cloud/data center capex growth view and introduces strong CY27 outlook, reinforcing a bullish stance on the semiconductor AI value chain.
reports.summary: J.P. Morgan argues AI infrastructure spend remains in an upward revision cycle, lifting its data center capex growth outlook to 63% for CY26 from 52% and introducing +40% growth for CY27. The note frames sustained hyperscaler and model-lab capacity constraints, rising inference and reasoning workload intensity, and growing backlog visibility at NVDA, AVGO, and MU as evidence that AI compute demand should continue supporting broad upside across semis, custom ASIC vendors, memory, semicaps, and EDA.
important_observations: JPM sees another year of upward capex revisions forming in 2026, similar to the pattern seen through 2025, implying consensus may still be too low for AI infra spend.
Custom AI ASICs are expected to gain share vs GPUs in CY26/CY27, creating a relative mix tailwind for AVGO and MRVL while still leaving overall accelerator demand very strong.
The report argues backlog and order-book visibility across semi coverage already points to robust CY27 demand, not just a short-cycle 2026 spike.

Installed North America DC power capacity growth is presented as a hard-asset constraint/supporting datapoint behind the spend outlook, reducing the risk that the capex thesis is purely narrative-driven.
JPM expects coming qtrs to show constructive commentary on customer activity, bookings, backlog, and supply-chain capacity, which could serve as incremental positive catalysts for AI-linked names.
new_data: CY26 data center capex growth forecast raised to 63% from 52%.
Initial CY27 data center capex growth forecast introduced at 40%.
More than $200bn of incremental data center capex implied in 2026, a record annual increase.
North American planned data center power capacity tracked at 140GW+ at end-2025.
Installed DC capacity forecast: 48GW in 2025, 60GW+ in 2026, 75GW+ in 2027.
JPM cites NVDA discussing $1T+ of Blackwell and Vera Rubin demand visibility through CY27.
JPM cites AVGO discussing $100B+ in AI rev in FY27.
JPM cites MU signing the industry's first five-year CSA.
management_insights: NVDA and AVGO commentary suggests extended visibility into CY27 as customers lock in capacity well ahead of deployment.
Hyperscalers are portrayed as remaining capacity-constrained and committed to scaling AI infrastructure despite already elevated spending.
Mgmt commentary cited by JPM supports continued GPU and XPU ramps over the next few qtrs.
Recent AVGO customer commitment and partnership commentary is used to support growing custom ASIC adoption.
The note frames inference-led demand and reasoning/agentic workloads as key drivers of rising compute intensity.

estimate_change_details.note: JPM revised CY26 growth outlook higher.
Initial CY27 forecast; no prior figure provided.
Forecast level disclosed, but no prior estimate given.
Forecast level disclosed, but no prior estimate given.

evidence_quality: Primary plus analyst synthesis: cites company-reported/backlog commentary from NVDA, AVGO, and MU alongside JPM sector views.
Triangulated with external market/industry data: 650 Group and Datacenter Hawk used for capex and capacity framing.
Mostly forward-looking analyst model work for CY26/CY27 capex and capacity forecasts, so conclusions depend on sustained hyperscaler follow-through.
Evidence base is stronger on demand visibility and capacity build plans than on exact downstream monetization by each covered stock.
Some conclusions on GPU vs custom ASIC mix shift are partly analyst inference, though supported by recent company commentary.
risks_or_watch_items: If hyperscaler capex pacing slips or deployment timing pushes out, the 2026-27 upward revision thesis could moderate.
The bullish CY27 setup assumes limited inflation/commodity pressure; renewed cost inflation could alter spend efficiency and mix.
A faster mix shift toward custom ASICs could redistribute value within the AI stack even if total spend stays strong.
Backlog visibility may not fully translate into near-term shipments if supply-chain bottlenecks or power constraints emerge.
Watch upcoming commentary on bookings, backlog, supply capacity, and customer ramp timing for confirmation of continued estimate upside.
key_numbers: CY26 data center capex growth: 63% vs prior 52%
North America planned DC power capacity at end-2025: 140GW+
Installed DC capacity: 48GW in 2025, 60GW+ in 2026, 75GW+ in 2027
NVDA demand visibility through CY27: $1T+ for Blackwell and Vera Rubin
AVGO AI rev discussed for FY27: $100bn+
